title: Conjecture on Planar Hat Guessing Number (Bosek et al. [4])
statement_text: The maximum possible hat guessing number of a planar graph is $4$.
context_text: Bosek, Dudek, Farnik, Grytczuk, and Mazur conjectured that $\mathrm{HG}(G) \leq 4$ for every planar graph $G$. This conjecture is the primary motivation for the paper's first main result.
attributed_to: Bosek, Dudek, Farnik, Grytczuk, and Mazur
paper_contribution: Disproves the conjecture by constructing a planar graph $G$ with $\mathrm{HG}(G) = 12$.
paper_title: On the Hat Guessing Number of Graphs

title: Conjecture on Hat Guessing Number vs. Hadwiger Number (Bosek et al. [4])
statement_text: The hat guessing number of any graph is at most its Hadwiger number, that is, the order of the largest clique minor of the graph.
context_text: Bosek et al. conjectured that $\mathrm{HG}(G)$ is bounded above by the Hadwiger number of $G$. The paper notes that the planar graph with $\mathrm{HG}(G)=12$ already provides a counterexample, and that for larger Hadwiger numbers $d$ the hat guessing number can in fact be at least doubly-exponential in $d$.
attributed_to: Bosek, Dudek, Farnik, Grytczuk, and Mazur
paper_contribution: Disproves the conjecture; the planar graph constructed has $\mathrm{HG}(G)=12$ while planar graphs exclude $K_5$ as a minor, giving Hadwiger number at most $4$.
paper_title: On the Hat Guessing Number of Graphs

title: Question on Degeneracy Bound for Hat Guessing Number (Alon et al. [1])
statement_text: Does there exist a function $f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ such that if $G$ is $d$-degenerate, then $\mathrm{HG}(G) \leq f(d)$?
context_text: This question was raised in [1] (Alon, Ben-Eliezer, Shangguan, Tamo). A positive answer would imply that the hat guessing number of any planar graph is bounded by an absolute constant (since planar graphs are $5$-degenerate) and that $\mathrm{HG}(G)$ is bounded by a function of the Hadwiger number (since graphs with Hadwiger number $k$ are $O(k\sqrt{\log k})$-degenerate).
paper_contribution: The paper restates the question and notes that a positive answer seems plausible, but does not prove it.
paper_title: On the Hat Guessing Number of Graphs

title: Informal Belief on Degeneracy Bound for Hat Guessing Number
statement_text: It seems plausible that there exists a function $f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ such that for every $d$-degenerate graph $G$, $\mathrm{HG}(G) \leq f(d)$.
context_text: The authors remark in the open problems section that such a bounding function seems plausible, noting that it would imply absolute constant bounds on the hat guessing number of planar graphs (which are $5$-degenerate) and a Hadwiger-number bound via degeneracy results.
paper_title: On the Hat Guessing Number of Graphs

title: Question on Typical Hat Guessing Number of G(n,1/2)
statement_text: What is the typical asymptotic behavior of the hat guessing number of the random graph $G(n,1/2)$? In particular, is $\mathrm{HG}(G(n,1/2))$ equal to $o(n)$ with high probability?
context_text: The paper improves the known lower bound from $(2-o(1))\log_2 n$ to $n^{1-o(1)}$ with high probability, but the true order of growth remains unknown. The upper bound is $n$ trivially.
paper_title: On the Hat Guessing Number of Graphs

title: Problem on Hat Guessing Number After Adding a Universal Vertex
statement_text: Establish an upper bound for $\mathrm{HG}(G')$ as a function of $\mathrm{HG}(G)$, where $G'$ is obtained from $G$ by adding a single vertex connected to all vertices of $G$, or show that no such function exists.
context_text: Using the graphs $G_d(N)$ from [10], the authors show that for arbitrarily large $q = \mathrm{HG}(G)$ the hat guessing number of $G'$ can exceed $q^2$ (specifically reaching $q^2+q$), so adding a universal vertex can dramatically increase the hat guessing number.
paper_title: On the Hat Guessing Number of Graphs
